summary: Gruber notices that Dropbox has renamed its main app to 'Dropbox: Cloud Photo Storage' in the App Store purely for SEO purposes, not because it's a dedicated photo app. He traces this to a broader trend where App Store entrepreneurs explicitly recommend stuffing keywords into app names to rank higher in search results. Gruber finds the practice depressing and argues that Apple's App Store search engine is to blame for rewarding keyword spamming rather than discouraging it. He doesn't fault developers for gaming a broken system, but holds Apple responsible for the perverse incentives.
key_points: Dropbox renamed its standard app to include 'Cloud Photo Storage' purely as an App Store SEO tactic, not reflecting any product change
App Store consultants explicitly recommend prepending keywords before the actual app name to boost search rankings
Apple's App Store search algorithm rewards keyword spamming rather than discouraging it
Gruber distinguishes between blaming developers who exploit the system and blaming Apple for creating the incentive structure
money_quotes: I quickly determined that this was just the regular Dropbox app. Dropbox has simply renamed it to include 'Cloud Photo Storage' in the name for SEO purposes.
The App Store should discourage SEO nonsense like keyword spamming, not reward it.
I don't blame developers for using unseemly naming tricks that work; I blame Apple for running a search engine that rewards such chicanery.
I find this unsurprising but depressing.
I disable the option for automatic updates to apps installed via the App Store on all my devices.
key_insight: Apple deserves the blame for App Store keyword spam because its search algorithm rewards the chicanery rather than discouraging it.
